title: The box and the ring
why.no-div: There is still no group. The image, the heading and the price are three separate things — but they are one bike. Wrap them.
why.no-class: The group is there, but it is nameless. `<div>` means nothing on its own — all its meaning comes from the label you give it.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: Two listings. Each is an image, a heading and a price. To the browser these are six independent things, stacked one under the other.
blocks.1.text: Tomorrow you will want each listing in a frame, with a background, with space around it. But there is nothing to grab — there is no "listing", there are three separate tags.
blocks.4.text: <div> is a box. It wraps several things and makes them one.
blocks.5.text: Now there is something to grab. You put a frame on the box and everything inside is framed.
blocks.8.text: <span> is the same, but inline — it lives inside the line and does not break it. It wraps part of a sentence.
blocks.9.text:
Remember and

? The same pair:

is block, is inline.
blocks.11.text: Here is the difference from everything so far. <h1> means heading. <ul> means list. <nav> means menu.
blocks.12.text: <div> means NOTHING. <span> means nothing. They are empty boxes whose only purpose is to give you something to grab.
blocks.13.text: That is exactly why they are dangerous. It is easy to wrap everything in them and tell yourself "it works". It works — but your code no longer says anything about what the page IS.
blocks.14.text: The rule: use <div> when there is REALLY no tag for what you are building.
blocks.15.text: The next lesson is about what happens when you forget that rule.
blocks.16.text: Your turn. Make each listing one thing.
